examples.title: Log-Based Recovery Algorithm
examples.description: Simplified recovery algorithm showing redo and undo phases
examples.code:
RECOVERY_ALGORITHM
BEGIN
 // Phase 1: Analysis
 Initialize TransactionTable as empty
 Initialize DirtyPageTable as empty
 Scan log forward from last checkpoint
 FOR each log record LSN do
 IF LSN.type == BEGIN THEN
 Add transaction to TransactionTable with status=active
 ELSE IF LSN.type == UPDATE THEN
 Update transaction's lastLSN in TransactionTable
 Add page to DirtyPageTable if not present
 ELSE IF LSN.type == COMMIT THEN
 Mark transaction as committed in TransactionTable
 ELSE IF LSN.type == ABORT THEN
 Mark transaction as aborted in TransactionTable
 END
 END

 // Phase 2: Redo
 FOR each page in DirtyPageTable do
 position = page.recoveryLSN
 Scan log from position
 REPEAT
 IF record.type == UPDATE THEN
 Redo the update (apply new value to page)
 END
 UNTIL end of log
 END

 // Phase 3: Undo
 WHILE there are active transactions do
 Select transaction with highest lastLSN
 IF lastLSN is UPDATE record THEN
 Generate CLR (Compensation Log Record)
 Undo the update (apply old value)
 Update transaction's lastLSN to previousLSN
 ELSE IF lastLSN is BEGIN record THEN
 Generate ABORT record
 Mark transaction as aborted
 Remove from active list
 END
 END
 RETURN "Recovery Complete"
END
examples.explanation: This algorithm shows the three phases of ARIES recovery: Analysis builds transaction and dirty page tables, Redo reapplies committed updates, and Undo reverses uncommitted transactions using compensation log records.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n log n) where n is number of log records
examples.spaceComplexity: O(t + d) where t=transactions, d=dirty pages

examples.title: Write-Ahead Logging Protocol
examples.description: Implementation of WAL for transaction commit
examples.code:
// Write-Ahead Logging Protocol
TRANSACTION_BEGIN(transaction_id)
BEGIN
 writeLog(BEGIN, transaction_id)
 RETURN transaction_id
END

TRANSACTION_WRITE(transaction_id, page_id, old_value, new_value)
BEGIN
 // Step 1: Write update record to log (must be first)
 writeLog(UPDATE, transaction_id, page_id, old_value, new_value)
 flushLogToDisk() // Force write to stable storage
 
 // Step 2: Modify the data page in buffer
 buffer[page_id] = new_value
 markPageDirty(page_id)
 
 RETURN "Update recorded"
END

TRANSACTION_COMMIT(transaction_id)
BEGIN
 // Step 1: Write commit record to log
 writeLog(COMMIT, transaction_id)
 flushLogToDisk() // Critical: must flush before returning success
 
 // Step 2: Optionally flush dirty pages
 // (can be deferred but log must be on disk)
 
 RETURN "Transaction committed"
END

TRANSACTION_ABORT(transaction_id)
BEGIN
 // Undo all updates for this transaction
 WHILE hasUndoableUpdates(transaction_id) DO
 update_record = getLastUpdate(transaction_id)
 
 // Write CLR before undoing
 writeLog(CLR, transaction_id, update_record)
 
 // Restore old value
 buffer[update_record.page_id] = update_record.old_value
 markPageDirty(update_record.page_id)
 
 advanceTransactionToPreviousLSN(transaction_id)
 END
 
 writeLog(ABORT, transaction_id)
 flushLogToDisk()
 
 RETURN "Transaction aborted and rolled back"
END
examples.explanation: This code demonstrates the WAL protocol where log records must be written to stable storage BEFORE any data modifications. Commit cannot return success until COMMIT record is persisted.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) per operation, O(n) for abort where n=updates

examples.title: Checkpoint Procedure
examples.description: Creating a fuzzy checkpoint in database
examples.explanation: Checkpoint creates a recovery point by saving transaction state and flushing dirty pages. Fuzzy checkpointing allows concurrent transactions during the process.
examples.timeComplexity: O(d) where d=number of dirty pages

examples.title: Undo Operation with CLRs
examples.description: Undo with Compensation Log Records for crash-safe undo
examples.explanation: CLRs (Compensation Log Records) are special log records written during undo that ensure undo operations themselves are durable and survive crashes.
examples.timeComplexity: O(u) where u=number of updates to undo

examples.title: Media Recovery Procedure
examples.description: Recovery from disk failure using backup and logs
examples.code:
MEDIA_RECOVERY(backup_filename, log_files[])
BEGIN
 // Step 1: Stop all database operations
 stopDatabase()
 
 // Step 2: Restore from most recent backup
 display("Restoring from backup: " + backup_filename)
 restoreDatabaseFromBackup(backup_filename)
 
 // Step 3: Determine backup checkpoint location
 checkpoint_lsn = findLastCheckpointInBackup()
 
 // Step 4: Apply all log files from checkpoint onwards
 FOR each log_file IN log_files DO
 IF log_file.contains_checkpoint(checkpoint_lsn) THEN
 start_point = log_file.positionOf(checkpoint_lsn)
 ELSE
 start_point = log_file.start
 END
 
 // Redo phase: replay committed transactions
 FOR each record IN log_file FROM start_point TO end DO
 IF record.type == UPDATE AND record.was_committed THEN
 page = getPage(record.page_id)
 page.data = record.new_value
 writePageToDisk(page)
 END
 END
 END
 
 // Step 5: Verify database consistency
 IF verifyConsistency() THEN
 display("Media recovery successful")
 startDatabase()
 RETURN SUCCESS
 ELSE
 display("Recovery failed - inconsistent state")
 RETURN FAILURE
 END
END
examples.explanation: Media recovery restores database from backup and replays log files from the backup checkpoint to bring database to consistent state after disk failure.
examples.timeComplexity: O(b + l) where b=backup size, l=log size
